narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 2 (well below averages of about 5.7 and 5.5), LSAT score of 22.70 (far under the 36.70 average), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status, which is common among applicants. It calculated a 48% probability that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales just enough to reject your admission as a precautionary measure to ensure incoming students are likely to succeed. Among all these factors, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward bar passage because it's stronger than most applicants', showing you have solid academic capability from college. However, your Year 3 decile ranking was the next biggest factor driving the failure prediction, as being in the second decile signals weaker performance relative to peers that year, raising concerns about consistency heading into law school. Your LSAT score followed closely as another key pull toward failure risk, given how much lower it is than the norm, which the model sees as a hurdle for mastering the intense bar material. These top three elements—your strong undergrad GPA helping you, but offset by lower Year 3 deciles and LSAT—were the primary drivers outweighing other details like your family background or full-time plans. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile aligns with many who struggle due to those dips in recent school performance and test scores, even with your GPA edge. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background; it's the model highlighting patterns from thousands of similar cases. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average, strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or prep, and reapplying with updated credentials that address these gaps. Many students in your position have succeeded by targeting these areas, turning predictions around. Your above-average GPA gives you a great foundation—build on it strategically. With focused effort, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next cycle.
